name: Hagia Sophia
description: A towering masterpiece of Byzantine architecture, now serving as a mosque and one of the world's most visited monuments. Its immense dome and gold mosaics leave every visitor speechless.

name: Topkapi Palace
description: The grand administrative and residential palace of the Ottoman sultans for four centuries. Its treasury houses stunning imperial jewels and sacred relics.

name: Blue Mosque
description: Sultan Ahmed Mosque, famous worldwide for its six minarets and stunning interior adorned with over 20,000 hand-painted Iznik tiles in cool blue tones.

name: Basilica Cistern
description: An underground Byzantine water reservoir supported by 336 marble columns, featuring the eerie upside-down Medusa heads at its base. A true hidden underworld of Istanbul.

name: Galata Tower
description: A medieval stone tower rising above the rooftops of Galata, offering panoramic 360-degree views of Istanbul and the Bosphorus. Built by the Genoese in 1348.

name: Dolmabahçe Palace
description: The last imperial palace of the Ottoman Empire, a European-style marvel on the Bosphorus shore boasting the world's largest Bohemian crystal chandelier. Atatürk spent his final days here.

name: Pandeli Restaurant
description: An Istanbul institution since 1901, nestled inside the Egyptian Bazaar with tiled Ottoman interiors. Famous for its lamb stew and böreks beloved by locals for generations.

name: Çiya Sofrası
description: A legendary Kadıköy restaurant preserving Anatolian village recipes that are disappearing elsewhere. The meze selection alone attracts food researchers from around the world.

name: Karaköy Güllüoğlu
description: Istanbul's most celebrated baklava house, serving paper-thin filo pastry soaked in clarified butter and pistachio since 1949. The lines are always worth it.

name: Vefa Bozacısı
description: A historic boza house open since 1876, still serving the fermented grain drink from the same marble counters. Atatürk himself was a customer — his glass is still on display.

name: Hafız Mustafa
description: One of Istanbul's oldest confectionery chains, beloved for its lokum (Turkish delight), künefe, and freshly baked simit served with clotted cream.

name: Mandabatmaz
description: A tiny, legendary coffee shop on a back alley in Beyoğlu serving the thickest, most intense Turkish coffee in the city. No table — just a stone ledge and pure coffee culture.

name: Petra Roastery
description: A specialty coffee pioneer in Istanbul's Cihangir neighborhood, roasting single-origin beans and offering expert pour-overs in a cozy, light-filled space.

name: Walter's Coffee Roastery
description: A quirky Breaking Bad–themed cafe on the Asian side of Istanbul with industrial interiors and exceptional cold brew. A cult favorite among the city's coffee aficionados.

name: Güllüoğlu Karaköy Café
description: The sit-down café annex of the famous baklava house, serving pastries alongside excellent Turkish tea and coffee with a Bosphorus glimpse.

name: Istanbul Modern
description: Turkey's first modern and contemporary art museum, housed in a striking building on the Bosphorus. It showcases landmark works by Turkish artists alongside international exhibitions.

name: Pera Museum
description: A distinguished private museum in Beyoğlu housing Orientalist paintings, Anatolian weights and measures, and the iconic Tortoise Trainer by Osman Hamdi Bey.

name: Rahmi Koç Museum
description: An extraordinary industrial museum on the Golden Horn filled with vintage cars, ships, aircraft, and steam engines — a paradise for history and technology enthusiasts.

name: Turkish & Islamic Arts Museum
description: Housed in the 16th-century İbrahim Paşa Palace, this museum holds one of the world's finest collections of Islamic calligraphy, carpets, and ethnographic objects.

name: Grand Bazaar
description: One of the world's oldest and largest covered markets, with over 4,000 shops selling carpets, jewelry, ceramics, and spices across 61 covered streets. A sensory labyrinth.

name: Egyptian Bazaar (Spice Bazaar)
description: A vibrant 17th-century market overflowing with spices, dried fruits, nuts, Turkish delight, and herbal teas. The aromas alone make it unmissable.

name: Arasta Bazaar
description: A quieter, more authentic alternative to the Grand Bazaar, steps from the Blue Mosque. Specializes in quality handmade ceramics, kilims, and Ottoman-style souvenirs.

name: Balat
description: A UNESCO-listed historic neighborhood of colorful crumbling houses, centuries-old synagogues, and hip cafes. Once home to Greek and Jewish communities, now a bohemian enclave.

name: Kuzguncuk
description: A charming Bosphorus village on the Asian shore where wooden houses painted in pastel colors line cobblestone streets. Beloved by artists and writers escaping the city's chaos.

name: Ortaköy Sahili
description: A scenic Bosphorus waterfront square dominated by the 19th-century Ortaköy Mosque and the Bosphorus Bridge. Famous for its waffle stalls and weekend craft markets.

name: Aşiyan Cemetery
description: A romantic hilltop cemetery above the Bosphorus village of Rumelihisarı, final resting place of Ottoman poets and intellectuals. Hauntingly beautiful and almost unknown.

name: Bosphorus Walk
description: A scenic waterfront promenade stretching along the European shore of the Bosphorus from Beşiktaş to Ortaköy. Spectacular views of Dolmabahçe Palace and Asian hills.

name: Balat–Fener Walk
description: A walking route through Istanbul's oldest Christian and Jewish neighborhoods, passing Byzantine churches, Ottoman fountains, and stairways draped in colorful laundry.

name: Prince Islands Trail
description: A bicycle and carriage trail circling Büyükada, the largest of Istanbul's car-free Princes Islands. Pine forests, belle époque villas, and sea views at every turn.

name: Belgrad Forest
description: Istanbul's green lung — a vast oak and beech forest north of the city with cycling paths, Ottoman aqueducts, and reservoirs. Perfect for weekend escapes from urban life.

name: Emirgan Park
description: Istanbul's most beloved public park on the Bosphorus, home to three 19th-century wooden kiosks and famous for its explosive tulip displays every April.

name: Princes Islands
description: A chain of nine car-free islands in the Sea of Marmara, reachable by ferry from the city. Pine-scented walks, pebble beaches, and a welcome escape from Istanbul's density.

name: Babylon Istanbul
description: Istanbul's most respected live music venue, hosting international and Turkish artists across jazz, rock, and electronic genres. A cornerstone of the city's nightlife since 1999.

name: Bant Mag Bahçe
description: An open-air bar and music garden in Karaköy run by the Turkish music magazine Bant Mag. Attracts the city's indie crowd with eclectic programming and laid-back garden vibes.

name: Arkaoda
description: A cult Kadıköy venue spreading across multiple floors with live music, DJs, a bookshop, and rooftop terrace. The heartbeat of Istanbul's alternative nightlife scene.

name: Agora of Smyrna
description: Remarkably preserved ruins of the ancient Roman agora lying right in the heart of modern Izmir. Stoa colonnades, vaulted arcades, and open-air excavation pits make it a must.

name: Kemeraltı Bazaar
description: Izmir's ancient bazaar stretching through a labyrinth of caravanserais, mosques, and covered passages. A living museum of trade where vendors sell herbs, fabrics, and antiques.

name: Kordon Promenade
description: Izmir's beloved seafront promenade lined with palm trees and historic buildings, stretching along the Aegean shore. Best enjoyed at sunset with a glass of Aegean wine.

name: Kadifekale
description: The ancient Velvet Castle perched on a hill above Izmir with sweeping views of the bay. Built by Lysimachos, the Hellenistic walls still stand among scattered wildflowers.

name: Alsancak Gastronomy Strip
description: Izmir's most vibrant dining street lined with Aegean mezze houses, fresh seafood restaurants, and cocktail bars. The city's equivalent of a great food boulevard.

name: Boyoz at Sevinç Pastanesi
description: The best place in Izmir to taste boyoz, the flaky Sephardic pastry unique to the city. This historic bakery has been perfecting the recipe since the 1950s.

name: Ephesus Ancient City
description: One of the best-preserved ancient cities in the Mediterranean, with the Library of Celsus, marble-paved streets, and a theatre seating 25,000. A day trip from Izmir.

name: Çeşme Beaches
description: Crystal-clear Aegean coves and windsurfing beaches just outside Izmir. Ilıca and Altınkum beaches offer turquoise shallows and thermal springs right at the shore.

name: Izmir Archaeology Museum
description: Houses thousands of artifacts from excavations across the Aegean region including the famous Apollo and Demeter statues from the Agora. A compact but world-class collection.

name: Kaleiçi Old Quarter
description: Antalya's charming walled old city, a maze of Roman, Byzantine, and Ottoman layers with marble alleys, restored mansions, and restaurants set in ancient courtyards.

name: Hadrian's Gate
description: A magnificent triumphal arch built in 130 AD to honor Emperor Hadrian's visit to Antalya. Three arched marble gateways frame the entrance to the old city with startling grace.

name: Düden Waterfall
description: A dramatic waterfall where the Düden River plunges directly into the Mediterranean Sea. The viewing platform lets you stand behind the curtain of falling water.

name: Konyaaltı Beach
description: A long pebble beach stretching beneath the Taurus Mountains with crystal Mediterranean waters. Backed by a modern park with bike paths, beach clubs, and the city skyline.

name: Antalya Archaeological Museum
description: One of Turkey's finest museums, with halls of Roman-era sarcophagi, the magnificent Gallery of the Gods, and treasures from Perge and Aspendos.

name: Perge Ancient City
description: A remarkably intact Greco-Roman city just east of Antalya, featuring colonnaded avenues, a theatre, and a stadium that once held 12,000 spectators.

name: Liman Lokantası
description: A historic workers' canteen by the harbor that has been serving Antalya fishermen and locals for decades with honest Anatolian food at budget prices.

name: Lycian Way Coastal Trail
description: A section of Turkey's famous long-distance hiking path following clifftop trails above the turquoise Mediterranean, passing Lycian rock tombs and hidden bays.

name: Mermerli Café & Beach
description: A secret café and tiny private beach hidden below Kaleiçi's walls, accessible through a tunnel. Known only to locals who prize it for sunset cocktails over calm water.

name: Colosseum
description: The colossal Flavian amphitheatre, capable of holding 80,000 spectators, where gladiators fought and emperors staged elaborate public spectacles. A timeless symbol of Rome.

name: Vatican Museums
description: A vast complex of papal collections including the Sistine Chapel, the Gallery of Maps, and Raphael Rooms. Over 54 galleries hold one of the world's greatest art treasures.

name: Trevi Fountain
description: Rome's grandest Baroque fountain, where Neptune commands the waters at the junction of three ancient aqueducts. Toss a coin and you are guaranteed to return to Rome.

name: Pantheon
description: The best-preserved ancient Roman building, with its unreinforced concrete dome and 9-metre oculus open to the sky. Still standing after nearly 2,000 years.

name: Roman Forum & Palatine Hill
description: The beating heart of ancient Rome, where Julius Caesar was assassinated, Mark Antony gave his eulogy, and emperors lived in vast hilltop palaces above the city.

name: Trastevere Trattorias
description: Rome's most atmospheric neighbourhood for dining, with ivy-draped trattorias serving cacio e pepe, carbonara, and crispy supplì in candlelit cobblestone alleys.

name: Roscioli Salumeria con Cucina
description: A legendary Roman deli-restaurant that is simultaneously a butcher, cheese shop, wine cellar, and restaurant. The carbonara is considered by many the best in Rome.

name: Sant'Eustachio il Caffè
description: Perhaps Rome's most revered espresso bar, roasting beans the same way since 1938. The gran caffè with a secret recipe has been drawing pilgrims for generations.

name: Testaccio Market
description: Rome's most authentic covered food market, where chefs and Roman families buy produce, offal, cheese, and street food. The suppli stall is unmissable.

name: Borghese Gallery
description: Arguably the finest small museum in the world, housing Bernini's breathtaking sculptures and Caravaggio masterpieces in a 17th-century villa surrounded by a magnificent park.

name: Via Appia Antica
description: Walk the ancient cobblestones of the 312 BC Appian Way, lined with crumbling Roman tombs, catacombs, and pastoral views unchanged since antiquity.

name: Pigneto Neighbourhood
description: Rome's coolest working-class neighbourhood, the setting of Pasolini films and now home to aperitivo bars, vinyl record shops, and street art. The real Rome away from tourists.

name: Villa Borghese Gardens
description: Rome's most beloved public park, 80 hectares of landscape gardens, fountains, and pine trees in the heart of the city. Rent a rowboat, cycle, or simply picnic with Romans.

name: Eiffel Tower
description: Gustave Eiffel's 330-metre iron lattice masterpiece, built for the 1889 World's Fair and now the world's most visited monument. Breathtaking from any angle, magical at night.

name: The Louvre
description: The world's largest art museum, housing over 35,000 works including the Mona Lisa, Venus de Milo, and Winged Victory. A former royal palace whose glass pyramid is iconic.

name: Notre-Dame Cathedral
description: The Gothic masterpiece of medieval Paris, whose restoration after the 2019 fire is now complete. A forest of flying buttresses, gargoyles, and the most famous rose windows in the world.

name: Musée d'Orsay
description: A converted Beaux-Arts railway station housing the world's greatest collection of Impressionist art — Monet's Water Lilies, Renoir, Van Gogh, Degas, and more.

name: Montmartre & Sacré-Cœur
description: Paris's hilltop bohemian village, crowned by the white-domed Sacré-Cœur basilica. Wander vine-covered lanes where Picasso, Toulouse-Lautrec, and Renoir once lived.

name: Le Comptoir du Relais
description: The most coveted brasserie table in Paris, run by legendary chef Yves Camdeborde serving exquisite bistronomy in the heart of Saint-Germain-des-Prés.

name: Septime
description: Paris's most-booked natural wine bistro, serving market-driven bistronomie in a relaxed industrial setting in Bastille. Reservations required weeks in advance.

name: Café de Flore
description: Paris's most legendary café, where Sartre, de Beauvoir, and Picasso wrote and debated over café crème. Still serving exceptional croissants on Boulevard Saint-Germain.

name: Ten Belles
description: The coffee shop that launched Paris's specialty coffee revolution, serving Scandinavian-roasted single origins on the Canal Saint-Martin with avocado toasts and excellent pastries.

name: Marché Bastille
description: Paris's most beloved outdoor market on Boulevard Richard-Lenoir, running every Thursday and Sunday. Locals shop for seasonal vegetables, charcuterie, and artisan cheeses.

name: Canal Saint-Martin Walk
description: A picturesque canal walk through Paris's trendiest neighbourhood, crossing iron footbridges and passing bateaux-lavoirs where locals picnic on the tree-lined embankments.

name: Bois de Boulogne
description: A vast 845-hectare forest park on the western edge of Paris with lakes, cycling paths, the Fondation Louis Vuitton museum, and the Roland-Garros tennis grounds.

name: Passage des Panoramas
description: Paris's oldest covered arcade, built in 1799 and still lined with stamp dealers, vintage print shops, and tiny restaurants. A hidden world untouched by mass tourism.

name: Rex Club
description: Paris's most iconic techno club, running in the basement of the Grand Rex cinema since 1981. A no-frills, dark room with an extraordinary sound system attracting the world's best DJs.
